title: Oral History Association Best Practices Guide
notes: OHA principles apply across all traditions. For First Nations content, OHA is supplemented by OCAP. For Inuit content, NISR compliance is additionally required. OHA principles: legal/ethical responsibility, interviewer competence, interviewee rights, community rights.

title: Smithsonian Folklife Documentary Standards
notes: Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage standards emphasize multi-generational context, living tradition documentation, and community-centered representation. Fieldwork norms align closely with OCAP for Indigenous content.

title: IQ-Pilimmaksarniq Oral Knowledge Transmission Guide
notes: This guide embeds the IQ principle of Pilimmaksarniq (learning through practice and observation) as its epistemological foundation. Oral history is not extraction — it is entering a learning relationship. The interviewer is an apprentice in the knowledge keeper's tradition, not a researcher harvesting data. All six IQ principles inform this guide: Inuuqatigiitsiarniq (respect), Tunnganarniq (openness), Pijitsirniq (service), Aajiiqatigiinniq (consensus), Pilimmaksarniq (practice-based learning), Piliriqatigiinniq (collective purpose).

title: Foxfire Student-Interviewer Method
notes: The Foxfire method (Eliot Wigginton, Rabun County, Georgia, 1966) positions the student as a genuinely curious learner and the elder as the expert authority. Key Foxfire principles: approach with openness not preconception; document the whole context not just the technique; the elder owns their story; community review validates accuracy. The Foxfire approach has deep parallels with IQ-Pilimmaksarniq — both treat learning as a relational process, not an extraction process.
